discovery.text: From Allan B. Jacobs' Great Streets (1993). The street of Fellini's La Dolce Vita — Jacobs examines it as a street with character beyond its fame.

narrative.summary: The street of Fellini's La Dolce Vita — Jacobs examines it as a street with character beyond its fame.

blocks.title: Why Jacobs Chose It
blocks.body: Elegant curved boulevard climbing from Piazza Barberini, lined with American embassy, grand hotels, pavement cafés under plane trees. The 1950s glamour is still present in the architecture even if the scene has moved on. The Capuchin Crypt at the bottom adds a macabre footnote.

blocks.title: How to Walk It
blocks.body: Walk the full length. Jacobs is explicit: you have to walk a great street to understand it — the sequence, the proportions, how the edges change. Give it an hour minimum. Sit at a café or on a bench midway. Notice what makes it feel alive.

blocks.name: Doney Restaurant & Café
blocks.address: Via Vittorio Veneto, 125, 00187 Roma RM, Italy
blocks.why: One of Via Veneto's classic hotel cafés, still sitting directly in the boulevard's theater of looking and being looked at. This is the seated street life Jacobs cares about, even if the glamour now feels more inherited than wild.

blocks.name: Hotel Eden
blocks.why: Just off Via Veneto at the top of the slope, this grande dame hotel helps explain the street's elegant edge condition. The luxury frontage and comings-and-goings keep the boulevard tied to Rome's old high-society geography.

blocks.name: Museo e Cripta dei Cappuccini
blocks.address: Via Vittorio Veneto, 27, 00187 Roma RM, Italy
blocks.why: At the Piazza Barberini end of Via Veneto, this odd and unforgettable museum gives the boulevard a distinctly Roman institutional anchor. It adds the kind of memorable termination and symbolic weight Jacobs notices in great streets.
